#introductions

2016-11-23

Sean Davis (08:14:52): > @Sean Davis has joined the channel

Sean Davis (08:14:53): > set the channel description: For members to introduce themselves as they join slack

Sean Davis (08:20:06): > About me: > - At the National Cancer Institute > - Bioconductor user since 2003 > - Pediatric oncologist > - Translational and basic genomics research areas, broadly > - Cloud technologies and cloud-scale genomics are recent interests > - Runner, 2 kids and a cat

Levi Waldron (08:21:48): > @Levi Waldron has joined the channel

Peter Hickey (08:21:48): > @Peter Hickey has joined the channel

Marcel Ramos Pérez (08:21:48): > @Marcel Ramos Pérez has joined the channel

Jack Zhu (08:21:48): > @Jack Zhu has joined the channel

Michael Lawrence (08:21:49): > @Michael Lawrence has joined the channel

Benjamin Haibe-Kains (08:21:49): > @Benjamin Haibe-Kains has joined the channel

Aedin Culhane (08:21:49): > @Aedin Culhane has joined the channel

Vince Carey (08:21:49): > @Vince Carey has joined the channel

Tim Triche (08:21:49): > @Tim Triche has joined the channel

Phil Chapman (08:21:49): > @Phil Chapman has joined the channel

Lucas Schiffer (08:21:49): > @Lucas Schiffer has joined the channel

Kasper D. Hansen (08:21:49): > @Kasper D. Hansen has joined the channel

Marcel Ramos Pérez (10:57:12): > A few quick points about me:
> * At the WaldronLab and Bioconductor > * MultiAssayExperiment package maintainer > * working on data for the curatedTCGAdata package > * interested in data representation (CN package in the works) > * tech enthusiast

Benjamin Haibe-Kains (17:43:49): > About me: > - At the Princess Margaret Cancer Centre > - Bioconductor user since 2004 > - Computer Scientist who is not really one anymore > - Translational research, omics biomarker discovery, pharmacogenomics > - Reproducible research > - Lazy runner, 2 kids > - Interest in renewable energy > - Still try apprehend the fact that Trump has been elected to rule US

2016-11-28

Nitesh Turaga (13:47:43): > @Nitesh Turaga has joined the channel

2016-12-13

Levi Waldron (16:45:02): > About me: > * assistant professor at the CUNY School of Public Health in NYC > * I teach introductory biostat and regression methods for our MPH program in epic/bios > * Bioconductor user only since about 2007 > * education was in physics and wood science > * involved in MultiAssayExperiment, curatedMetagenomicData, ovarian cancer subtype validation, validation of prediction models, and a few other things > * cyclist, coffee drinker > * still in denial about Trump

Nitesh Turaga (21:31:51): > About me: > - New addition to the Bioconductor Core team at Roswell Park. > - Previously a Software Engineer at the Galaxy Project at Johns Hopkins. > - Still figuring Buffalo, NY out. > - I like to travel, and Crossfit.

2016-12-15

Phil Chapman (08:55:42): > About me: > - At the University of Manchester in the UK working in the Drug Discovery Unit at the Cancer Research UK Manchester Institute > - Previously at AstraZeneca > - Bioconductor user since 2012 > - Originally a molecular biologist before moving into bioinformatics > - Interested in applying published pharmacogenomic methods to data we have generated on our preclinical compounds, also RNAseq, methylation, lots of things really - whatever it takes to move our projects forward towards the clinic and find/validate new drug discovery targets > - Like working with normalised/tidy data i.e. SQL db’s, tidyverse etc > - Kind of relieved Trump got elected as everyones stopped laughing at us about Brexit

Sean Davis (11:25:28): > @Phil Chapman, have you played with BigQuery much? It is really amazing technology and is really ripe for data integration with pharmacogenomics data. If you need an account and happen to be working on anything cancer-related, let me know.

Phil Chapman (11:40:08): > Thanks@Sean Davisthat sounds really interesting, i’ve heard@Vince Careytalk about it but not tried it myself. Have been mainly using@Benjamin Haibe-Kains’s PharmacoGx recently as it stores drug response data as well as genomic data and solves a number of issues such as cell line naming conventions.

Marcel Ramos Pérez (11:42:12): > @Phil Chapmanthat reminds me to work on the port frompSettoMultiAssayExperiment..

2017-04-26

Lori Shepherd (11:37:21): > @Lori Shepherd has joined the channel

2017-05-03

Artem Sokolov (10:33:47): > @Artem Sokolov has joined the channel

2017-05-19

Ludwig Geistlinger (15:29:49): > @Ludwig Geistlinger has joined the channel

Ludwig Geistlinger (15:30:07): > About me:

Ludwig Geistlinger (15:33:22): > - recently joined the lab of Levi Waldron at the CUNY School of Public Health in NYC

Ludwig Geistlinger (15:34:54): > - gene expression data analysis with a focus on gene sets, pathways, and networks

Ludwig Geistlinger (15:36:03): > - these days moving more towards multi-assay analysis

Ludwig Geistlinger (15:36:37): > - some other interests include sports, nature, and literature

Ludwig Geistlinger (15:38:18): > - to keep the flame burning: still amused that Trump refused to shake hands with Chancellor Merkel

2017-05-20

John Readey (13:59:53): > @John Readey has joined the channel

John Readey (14:00:13): > About me:

John Readey (14:00:47): > - have been working at The HDF Group for last two years

John Readey (14:01:25): > - interested in data services, cloud computing

John Readey (14:01:53): > - previously worked at Amazon/AWS

John Readey (14:02:35): > - other interests: sailing, chess

Sean Davis (14:03:10): > Great that you could join us,@John Readey.

2017-05-23

Aedin Culhane (12:21:40): > Sorry I missed this channel… About me

Aedin Culhane (12:21:58): > - Working in Bioconductor since early 2000s

Aedin Culhane (12:22:26): > Working in Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, Harvard TH Chan School of Public Health

Aedin Culhane (12:23:30): > - Previously in Ireland (UCC, UCD), UK (Univ Manchester, Pfizer, BBSRC in Harpenden), France (briefly.. worked with ade4 guys in Lyon)

Aedin Culhane (12:23:55): > - Interested in Data integration, dimension reduction methods, gene sets, single sample gsea methods, biclustering

Aedin Culhane (12:24:31): > - PanCancer analysis, TCGA, tumor microenvironment, neuro-immune, immune role in disease

David Jenkins (16:33:24): > @David Jenkins has joined the channel

2017-10-27

Guangchuang Yu (05:28:59): > @Guangchuang Yu has joined the channel

Guangchuang Yu (05:37:43): > I am Guangchuang YU. Recently got my PhD degree and now work as a postdoc in school of public health, the University of Hong Kong. I have contributed several pkgs and my favorite one is ggtree:blush:

Sean Davis (06:37:38): > Great to have you join us,@Guangchuang Yu.

Stian Lågstad (13:30:20): > @Stian Lågstad has joined the channel

2017-11-28

Stephanie Hicks (14:19:44): > @Stephanie Hicks has joined the channel

Simina Boca (14:25:12): > @Simina Boca has joined the channel

Simina Boca (14:25:39): > Hi all!:wave:

Simina Boca (14:26:07): > I’m an Assistant Professor in Biomed Informatics, Oncology, and Biostat at Georgetown

Simina Boca (14:26:23): > I have a few Bioc packages and would love to be more involved!

2017-11-29

Matthew McCall (09:35:13): > @Matthew McCall has joined the channel

Matthew McCall (09:41:25): > Hello all. I’m an Assistant Professor in Biostatistics and Computational Biology at University of Rochester. I have several BioC packages and more in the works. I’m currently working a lot with network-like data for which it would be useful to store information about pairs (possibly groups) of features rather than single individual features. Not sure if something like this already exists in BioC.

Ludwig Geistlinger (09:47:34): > Hi Matthew, thegraphpackage implements general representations for networks. On the other hand, theInteractionSetpackage is a more specific package for genomic interaction data. In case, you are missing functionality for your data, this would be interesting to discuss on bioc devel.

Sean Davis (09:48:58): > Hi,@Matthew McCall. Good to see you again.

Matthew McCall (09:54:27): > Hi Sean! I recalled hearing about this slack at the last BioC but was too busy to follow up at the time.@Stephanie Hicksgets credit for providing me the link to join.

Matthew McCall (09:57:29): > Hi Ludwig, we should probably move this conversation out of#introductionsbut I do use the graph package for some stuff. The limitation there is (to my knowledge) the inability to link the relational data to the marginal data (e.g. feature counts in a SummarizedExperiment). I wasn’t aware of the InteractionSet package. That looks closer to what I’m interested in, but I’ll need to dig more to see whether it would work. - Attachment: Attachment > Hi Matthew, the graph package implements general representations for networks. On the other hand, the InteractionSet package is a more specific package for genomic interaction data. In case, you are missing functionality for your data, this would be interesting to discuss on bioc devel.

Levi Waldron (09:59:05): > Hi@Matthew McCall! There’s been some related discussion in the#tree-like-sechannel here. Our application has been more specifically focused on microbiome data, but I have wondered whether the data class we’re discussing should be more general. Would be interested in your input.@natedolsonhas posted a draft class of an extension of DataFrame to represent phylogenetic trees, which are a network. The motivation for extending DataFrame is that it could then be used as rowData in a SummarizedExperiment.

Matthew McCall (10:05:28): > Hi@Levi Waldron. Yes, that’s exactly what I was thinking — that this info could be a special type of rowData if formatted correctly. I just joined the#tree-like-sechannel, and I’ll take a look at the DataFrame extension for phylo trees. Thanks!

Simina Boca (14:04:26): > Piggy-backing onto what@Matthew McCallsaid, if we have questions similar to his questions (e.g. “I would like this feature, but if it doesn’t exist I would be happy to help out”) what is the recommended decision tree of posting it here vs bioc-devel or elsewhere?

Lucas Schiffer (14:10:57): > @Simina BocaI don’t know that there is a consensus just yet but I see bioc-devel as more for something like “I am having a problem with X, can someone help me”. Then Slack is more about envisioning new things and cooking up ideas like#tree-like-se:wink:

Simina Boca (14:12:08): > Got it! Cool, thanks!

Simina Boca (14:14:03): > Many of my applied projects involve metabolomics, so if there’s a lot of interest in that, I would be happy to at least brainstorm some ideas around usability and some workflows in that space (I know at least@Kasper D. Hansenwork on some metabolomics too)

Levi Waldron (14:42:24) (in thread): > The Slack channel does seem to be more interactive around this kind of cooking up of ideas. That said, I think it doesn’t hurt to post that kind of question on bioc-devel as well, since I believe it has a larger membership.

Levi Waldron (14:44:39) (in thread): > I don’t have a specific interest around metabolomics, but as one of the authors of MultiAssayExperiment (http://bioconductor.org/packages/MultiAssayExperiment/), I’m interested in seeing that how different kinds of data are represented and making sure they’re compatible with MultiAssayExperiment! - Attachment (Bioconductor): MultiAssayExperiment > Multi-assay ’omics experiments on a set of samples are increasingly commonplace in biomedical research. MultiAssayExperiment implements data structures and methods for representing, manipulating, and integrating multi-assay experiments via efficient construction, subsetting, and extraction operations. These methods are implemented matching Bioconductor user experience by straightforward extending concept and design of single-assay classes such as SummarizedExperiment or ExpressionSet.

Simina Boca (15:51:47) (in thread): > Cool! Let’s keep in touch! You’re also funded through ITCR right? I just became an ITCR investigator in June.

2017-12-06

Levi Waldron (05:05:54) (in thread): > Yes I am! Sorry I didn’t see your reply until now:open_mouth:

Simina Boca (11:39:40) (in thread): > No worries! Threads can be easy to miss.

2017-12-08

Lucas Schiffer (14:16:20): > Cool! Nice to meet you@Federico Marini

Lluís Revilla (07:29:10): > I’m the developer ofhttps://doi.org/doi:10.18129/B9.bioc.BioCor, if you want to know more about it let me know - Attachment (Bioconductor): BioCor > Calculates functional similarities based on the pathways described on KEGG and REACTOME or in gene sets. These similarities can be calculated for pathways or gene sets, genes, or clusters and combined with other similarities. They can be used to improve networks, gene selection, testing relationships…

2018-12-19

Leo Lahti (04:37:00): > @Leo Lahti has joined the channel

Leo Lahti (04:40:55): > Hello Bioc. I just found my way in this Slack. Author of 4 bioc packages over the last 10 years, including the latest one, microbiome R package (http://microbiome.github.io/microbiome/). I met some of you at the European BIoc Meeting in Munich this year. More info on our research athttps://openresearchlabs.github.io/- currently my primary interests include combinations of machine learning & ecological models and multi-omics data integration in the context of microbiome research. - Attachment (openresearchlabs.github.io): Open Research Labs - Home > Open Research Labs, University of Turku, Finland.

2018-12-28

2019-04-25

Jake Taylor-King (06:28:06): > @Jake Taylor-King has joined the channel

Jake Taylor-King (06:35:02): > Hi all, I was hoping for some advice. I’m a bioinformatician typically working on RNA-Seq with patient data. > > However, a new project has come up working with DNA-Seq on a novel plant variety. We need someone who can construct a library for the genome from scratch, vaguely estimate genes of interest through homology between species, and thereafter choose genes for KO experiments (with some end phenotype in mind). They would also be working with experienced plant biologists. > > We’re in the process of writing a job advert. If anyone has 3 minutes, could you let me know the kind of packages/programmes I would be expecting someone to have experience in?

Jake Taylor-King (06:38:35): > Thanks in advance! Best, Jake

Vince Carey (14:46:43): > @Jake Taylor-Kinghave you considered posting your question tosupport.bioconductor.com? There is a much broader readership. The slack might well be highly biased towards applications to human genome.

Vince Carey (14:58:58): > On the other hand if you are talking about constructing Bioconductor packages (you mention “library for the genome” which can have various interpretations) it might be worthwhile to have a look at all the packages athttp://bioconductor.org/packages/release/BiocViews.html#___Arabidopsis_thaliana– this addresses a variety of annotation approaches relevant for a plant. The landscape of packages ranging from BSgenome* through TxDb* to hom* should be understood by anyone tackling the tasks you mention in a Bioc-oriented way.

2019-04-26

Jake Taylor-King (06:26:55): > Hi@Vince Carey- thanks for the message, much appreciated.:slightly_smiling_face:

2019-04-30

Pratima Chennuri (13:13:54): > @Pratima Chennuri has joined the channel

Nicholas Knoblauch (13:25:49): > Hi everybody, I’m Nick Knoblauch, a PhD student in the Genetics Genomics and Systems Biology program at the University of Chicago and a part-time consultant for the HDF group (the nonprofit behindHDF5). I’m a long timeHDF5andRuser in my own research (statistical molecular genetics) but I’d be curious to know more about the broaderR/HDF5ecosystem in the life sciences (e.g what do people like/not like?)

Tim Triche (15:44:24): > people would like HDF5 and restfulSE / HSDS a lot if they knew more about it, I think@Nicholas Knoblauch:slightly_smiling_face:

Tim Triche (15:45:22): > if you work with John Readey, please pass along my apologies for taking so damned long to get the TARGET multiAssayExperiments into a Restful form for HSDS. He has been very helpful and I am remiss for falling behind

Nicholas Knoblauch (16:30:13): > @Tim TricheI agree! I think@John Readeyis around the channel from time to time. One thing I’m particularly interested in helping out with around here is HSDS, so if there’s anything I can do to help let me know!

Tim Triche (16:31:21): > no good deed goes unpunished… will be corresponding with you next week I think:slightly_smiling_face:

2019-05-27

Justin (15:53:45): > @Justin has joined the channel

Ni Zhao (18:30:49): > @Ni Zhao has joined the channel

Vince Carey (20:11:09): > @Nicholas Knoblauchthere is a brief discussion of HSDS in relation to Bioconductor athttps://f1000research.com/articles/8-21/v1 - Attachment (f1000research.com): F1000Research Article: restfulSE: A semantically rich interface for cloud-scale genomics with Bioconductor. > Read the latest article version by Shweta Gopaulakrishnan, Samuela Pollack, BJ Stubbs, Hervé Pagès, John Readey, Sean Davis, Levi Waldron, Martin Morgan, Vincent Carey, at F1000Research.

Vince Carey (20:13:20): > @Tim Tricheonce your HDF5 for TARGET is in an S3 bucket there should be no problem getting it indexed in HSDS. Let me know the situation.

2019-05-28

Kirk Reardon (02:53:17): > @Kirk Reardon has joined the channel

Tim Triche (06:51:38): > I know – the deal with TARGET pAML is mostly that it has expanded so rapidly (@Vince Carey)

Tim Triche (06:54:44): > @Vince Carey: you and John and now@Nicholas Knoblauchhave been absurdly patient and I thank you for that. This week actually will be the end of the game, though; we’re resubmitting to Leukemia, Blood, and eventually (garbled glam pub) so it’s all I’m really doing these days. Also, thanks for the work on restfulSE – I noticed that an enormous number of large-scale studies are in there now! The TARGET pAML data is a huge MAE which will only get bigger with the WGS+WES (yes, both) data and DNA/RNA allelic imbalance results. I guess those are the only results that won’t be going in this week.

kipper fletez-brant (11:49:08): > Hi all, I am a computational biologist at 23andMe Therapeutics. I’ve been using Bioc for years, and have a few packages contributed. Much of my work right now is related to functional genomics. EDIT: I am also an alum of@Kasper D. Hansenlab

2019-08-15

Eric Fournier (09:38:48): > @Eric Fournier has joined the channel

Constantin Ahlmann-Eltze (11:13:48): > @Constantin Ahlmann-Eltze has joined the channel

Constantin Ahlmann-Eltze (11:19:23): > Hi, I am a new PhD student in Wolfgang Huber’s group at EMBL. I have been using BioConductor for a while now and also recently submitted my first package called proDA:slightly_smiling_face:

brejnev muhire (12:48:51): > @brejnev muhire has joined the channel

Boris Hejblum (18:44:08): > @Boris Hejblum has joined the channel

Boris Hejblum (19:02:40): > Hi, I’m an Assistant Professor of Biostatistics at the University of Bordeaux (France), and I intend to soon submit my first package to Bioconductor (dearseq).

2019-08-16

Mikhael Manurung (09:10:07): > Greetings everyone! I’m a PhD student at Leiden University Medical Center, the Netherlands. I have only been working with R/BioC for a year. However, it has been such a transforming experience and I am delighted to found such a welcoming community:slightly_smiling_face:.

Mikhael Manurung (09:11:03) (in thread): > Are you the one who made the vici package?

Boris Hejblum (09:11:29) (in thread): > Hi Mikhael

Boris Hejblum (09:11:36) (in thread): > Yes, I am.

2019-08-17

Mikhael Manurung (12:21:29) (in thread): > Thanks for making the package! I have tried to ask a statistician in my department to do something about background-subtracting practice when working with intracellular-cytokine staining data (some also set negative values to zero!), but no one really cared about it.

Boris Hejblum (20:17:29) (in thread): > Thanks, I’m glad you liked it ! A paper should be coming out soon. Any feedback on VICI is welcomed I added a few features last week

2019-08-18

Luke Zappia (19:34:26): > @Luke Zappia has joined the channel

2019-08-19

Mikhael Manurung (05:22:40) (in thread): > I look forward to the paper! If you don’t mind, could you elaborate on why you use gls instead of lme for the intra-arm fit? If we use lme then I can use subject ID as random effect to take into account the repeated measures, right?

Kin Lau (17:42:26): > @Kin Lau has joined the channel

2019-08-20

Al J Abadi (20:28:25): > @Al J Abadi has joined the channel

2019-08-23

Boris Hejblum (06:06:55) (in thread): > This is a good question ! nlme::gls is what lets us fit the bivariate model we propose (with distinct variances for background and signal). Adding a random effect to such a model could lead to numerical convergence issues when the number of individuals is not large enough (and in our phase I/II trials we often have only a couple dozen of patients). An additional reason no to is to facilitate interpretation of the results, in particular for our immunologist collaborators.

Tim Thurman (13:13:48): > @Tim Thurman has joined the channel

2019-08-24

Madison Tyler (09:33:22): > @Madison Tyler has joined the channel

2019-08-25

Xiaotong Wang (20:21:37): > @Xiaotong Wang has joined the channel

2019-08-26

Mikhael Manurung (10:34:08) (in thread): > That would be an issue indeed. However, what if we add information on the within-group correlation structure such as withcorrelation = corCompSymm(, form = ~1|ID)? In this way, we would avoid going into mixed models and decorrelate the residuals. What do you think?

Boris Hejblum (10:49:25) (in thread): > Well I think this formula fits a different variance for each individual, which would requires that you have enough measured time-points. In our case we typically have only two which is not nearly enough to get stable estimates.

Mikhael Manurung (11:04:14) (in thread): > Thanks for the prompt response. If one has enough time points, would the approach I describe previously be the better one? > > By the way, could you point me to a reference about sample size/time point requirement to get stable estimates? Thanks again for sharing your insights.

Boris Hejblum (11:11:20) (in thread): > It’s hard to talk of “the better” approach. Your proposed model would have more flexibility because it has more parameters, but the larger question would be whether this flexibility would be useful to answer your scientific question of interest. In the case of a large inter-individual variability it would probably be nice to to incorporate that in the model, as you propose to do. > > Now concerning sample-size/time points requirements I’m not sure there is a precise answer. The minimum to be able to compute a variance is two points, so you definitely need more than that to get a reliable estimate…

2019-08-28

Aditya Bhagwat (10:05:14): > @Aditya Bhagwat has joined the channel

Ana Beatriz Villaseñor Altamirano (17:47:58): > @Ana Beatriz Villaseñor Altamirano has left the channel

Andrea Rau (09:10:14): > @Andrea Rau has joined the channel

Andrea Rau (09:20:32): > Hi all! I’m a long-time BioC user + developer (HTSFilter and coseq) and first-time Slack user:wink:, working as a researcher at the French National Institute for Agricultural Research in France in animal/plant genomics. I got motivated to check out the Slack channel after a great experience at#eurobioc2019(thanks@Laurent Gattofor organizing and@Federico Marinifor getting me set-up on Slack!). Looking forward to following discussions and getting involved in the community.

2019-12-12

Emma Jablonski (04:08:12): > @Emma Jablonski has joined the channel

2019-12-13

Teun van den Brand (14:18:25): > @Teun van den Brand has joined the channel

Teun van den Brand (14:35:27): > Hi everyone, I’m a medium-time (+-2 years) BioC user and aspiring contributor. I work as a computational PhD student on chromatin biology/gene regulation at the Netherlands Cancer Institute, coincidentally located at the eponymous country. I too wanted to probe the Slack channel due to the great#eurobioc2019conference. If the past is any guarantee of the future and since I’m new to slack, I’ll likely lurk around here and follow some discussions with ditto interest.

2019-12-15

Paul Harrison (20:13:09): > @Paul Harrison has joined the channel

Paul Harrison (20:22:58): > Hi everyone. I’m with the Bioinformatics Platform at Monash University. I’ve contributed one package to Bioconductor, topconfects, which allows for differential expression analysis based on confidence bounds. Currently working a package for creation/calibration/component-extraction of matrices with precision weights. I also run a series of introductory R workshops at my university.

2019-12-20

I’m currently dipping my fingers more and more into analysis for spatial cytometry technologies.

Giuseppe D’Agostino (07:24:13): > @Giuseppe D’Agostino has joined the channel

Giuseppe D’Agostino (07:26:27): > Hi all, I’m Giuseppe, a postcoctoral researcher in Sarah Langley’s lab at LKC Medical School in Singapore. I did my wet lab PhD in Milan befriending my then colleague@Pierre-Luc Germainand then moved on to computational biology. I dabble in analysis and some methods development for single cell RNA-seq, in the context of neurodegenerative and neurodevelopmental disorders. Glad to be here!

2020-03-20

Peter Allen (14:42:36): > @Peter Allen has joined the channel

2020-03-23

Edgar (10:15:03): > @Edgar has joined the channel

Edgar (10:21:16): > Hello everybody, I’m Edgar, a postdoctoral researched at Icahn School of Medicine in NYC. I’m a biologist. PhD in bioinformatics. I work primarily with transcriptomics in cancer and infectious diseases. Nice meeting you all.

Sean Davis (10:31:26): > Welcome,@Edgar

brian capaldo (13:02:43): > @brian capaldo has joined the channel

brian capaldo (13:11:31): > Hello all, I am a bioinformatics scientist at the NCI working in prostate cancer. I’ve been doing this for about a decade and currently do a lot of single cell

Sridhar N (13:17:03) (in thread): > OK

brian capaldo (14:54:21): > thanks

Sridhar N (15:05:11) (in thread): > lol

Sridhar N (15:05:37) (in thread): > you guys don’t like our hospitality

Tamas Kiss (05:23:49): > @Tamas Kiss has joined the channel

Paula Restrepo (09:27:43): > @Paula Restrepo has joined the channel

Paula Restrepo (09:32:46): > Hi all! I’m a bioinformatician at Mount Sinai in NYC, I’m pretty early career (in one those weird gap year things between undergrad and phd), and I work with a lot of cancer transcriptomics and lately some more RRBS stuff.

Sridhar N (10:27:56) (in thread): > That’s it@Paula Restrepois here to

Sridhar N (10:28:00) (in thread): > Im out

Paula Restrepo (10:28:05) (in thread): > hey!

Paula Restrepo (10:28:37) (in thread): > alternatively, does this mean the takeover is complete?

brian capaldo (10:29:01) (in thread): > only when fejes joins a slack dedicated to R

Paula Restrepo (10:29:18) (in thread): > lmao true

Sridhar N (10:29:55) (in thread): > blasphemy

Paula Restrepo (10:33:39) (in thread): > no, this is just a lot of joking around

Paula Restrepo (10:34:09) (in thread): > the slack workspace from the bioinfo subreddit is much higher quality than the subreddit itself though if you’re interested in joining it

Sean Davis (10:42:04) (in thread): > Hi, Brian. Welcome to where the real work happens!

Sean Davis (10:42:33) (in thread): > What group are you with at NCI,@brian capaldo?

brian capaldo (10:42:40) (in thread): > LGCP

brian capaldo (10:43:35) (in thread): > no worries

2020-04-30

Chase Clark (13:06:48): > Hello, I’m grad student in Chicago and have written a couple of R packages (chasemc.github.io/electricShine) (https://chasemc.github.io/IDBac/). > In the not-too-distant future I’ll be transitioning from mostly metabolomics to metagenomics/metatranscriptomics/genomics and would love suggestions of reading material for transitioning to python from R but also blogs/etc on current Bioc tools for those fields.

Nicholas Knoblauch (13:23:09): > If you’ve already written R packages this may be a bit basic, but I usually point python programmers towardshttps://tidynomicon.tech/ - Attachment (tidynomicon.tech): The Tidynomicon > The Tidynomicon

Chase Clark (14:31:26) (in thread): > Thanks, I’m actually looking for the opposite direction, python for someone who primarily programs in R

Nicholas Knoblauch (14:54:06) (in thread): > oops. As someone who does transcriptomics/genomics and uses R as my main scripting language I’ve found that unlike R, a lot of the bioinformatics tools written in python are meant to be run from the command line (the genomics community has a pretty intense flat text fetish) , and don’t actually require much python knowledge. That being said, snakemake is a pretty excellent workflow management tool that requires almost no python knowledge to start using (and is also very R friendly)

2020-05-01

michaelkleymn (09:02:05): > @michaelkleymn has joined the channel

2020-05-03

Nitin Sharma (11:11:22): > Hello everyone, Nitin here. > > I have a PhD in structural bioinformatics from the National University of Singapore. I have done postdocs in Cambridge and then CRUK-MI, where I worked in the analysis of RNA-Seq data mainly, with some exposure to Methylation data and Single-Cell RNA-seq analysis. I am currently working as a postdoc at the Institute of Cancer Research, Oslo to understand the drug resistance and cancer evolution in the gastrointestinal stromal tumour (GIST) using RNA-Seq, microarray and whole-exome analysis and Single-cell analysis. > > I will be more active in single cell domain from now on and have created a channel#singlecell-queriesfor more general queries regarding single-cell analysis.

Shuyu Zheng (09:59:29): > Hi everyone, > I’m a Ph.D. student in University of Helsinki. I’m working on drug combination screen data and maintaining the R packagesynergyfinder. I just started a project in the single cell domain. - Attachment (Bioconductor): synergyfinder > Efficient implementations for all the popular synergy scoring models for drug combinations, including HSA, Loewe, Bliss and ZIP and visualization of the synergy scores as either a two-dimensional or a three-dimensional interaction surface over the dose matrix.

brian capaldo (10:46:54): > My entire grad school focused on synergy and now I find out about this package????

Shuyu Zheng (11:37:02) (in thread): > It seems that we should have built it earlier.:smile:Anyway, welcome to try and comment it.

Robert Herczeg (09:04:10): > @Robert Herczeg has joined the channel

Ruben Dries (12:27:40): > @Ruben Dries has joined the channel

Ruben Dries (16:07:27): > Hi all, I’m currently a postdoc at DFCI/Harvard in GC Yuan’s lab and I will soon (August 1st) open the doors of my own lab at BUMC. I’m specifically interested in understanding developmental and cancer dynamics. Therefore I like to combine R data analysis with experimental approaches and state-of-the-art of functional genomics, like TT-seq, polyA-seq, HiChIP, … . In addition, I also have a particular interest in single-cell and spatial transcriptomic analysis and visualization. I look forward to contribute to the Bioc Community in the future!

Jason (18:49:35): > @Jason has joined the channel

2020-06-18

pamela himadewi (09:54:43): > @pamela himadewi has joined the channel

Camila Daiana Arcuschin (10:15:57): > @Camila Daiana Arcuschin has joined the channel

2020-06-19

Jim Hunter (14:01:53): > @Jim Hunter has joined the channel

Jelmer Poelstra (15:13:36): > @Jelmer Poelstra has joined the channel

Atul Deshpande (17:06:24): > @Atul Deshpande has joined the channel

Atul Deshpande (17:32:15): > Hi all, I am a postdoc at Johns Hopkins in the Fertig Lab. I am interested in understanding intercellular interactions within the TME, hopefully through spatial omics data. As a red-green colorblind person, and given that visualizations are a big part of research communication in this field, I am also extremely interested in helping the drive to make default visualizations colorblind friendly. To illustrate my point, in the Ishihara test here, normal vision people see the number 74, and I (and a lot of colorblind people) see the number 21. With this kind of disparity in pattern recognition when relying on solely colors, it is not only important to me to have access to colorblind friendly palettes while doing my own analyses, but also to promote colorblind friendly practices in the community so that more and more research is accessible to colorblind people. I look forward to brainstorm on this as well as contribute to other bioc community initiatives in the future. - File (PNG): Ishihara_9.png

Simina Boca (18:38:39): > At least 5% of the population is color-blind. When I was in grad school (also at Hopkins!) there were at least 2 people in the genomics group who were colorblind, which meant they couldn’t tell apart the “standard” red/green heatmaps.

Dan Bunis (18:47:46) (in thread): > I don’t even see the 21! I guess we have a different form of red-green colorblindness.

Atul Deshpande (18:48:27) (in thread): > I keep switching between 21 and 81 depending on my mood.:slightly_smiling_face:

2020-06-20

Massimiliano Manzo (02:26:55): > @Massimiliano Manzo has joined the channel

nordwave (06:13:22): > @nordwave has joined the channel

Jim Hunter (08:28:30): > Hi, I’m Jim Hunter, an Adjunct Professor in Ricardo Diaz’ Retrovirology Lab at the Paulista School of Medicine, part of the Federal University of São Paulo in Brazil. My focus is on mutational patterns in HIV and how that affects treatments and a potential cure. Of course, this year, like everyone else, I’m spending more time on COVID-19 disease dynamics since we clearly don’t have a grip on this in Brazil.

I’ve noticed that Bioconductor conference will soon be starting in July. Do you think that the participating as attendee would be suitable/useful for me? thank you! Serena

Kirill Tsyganov (18:54:53): > @Kirill Tsyganov has joined the channel

Kirill Tsyganov (19:30:26): > Hey people, thanks to@Aedin Culhanefor introducing me to this community - I had no idea such cool place existed. I’m from down under (Melbourne, Australia) with Russian root:smile:. I work at the Monash University, at the Monash Bioinformatics Platform. It’s too long to explain what the platform is about, but in the nutshell we are slightly bigger than just a “service providers”. Apart from working with researchers on solving biological problems with the data, we also do a hole lot of community engagement and outreach. > Myself - I essentially work with any kind of genomics data, anything that comes of sequencing machines. I’m trying to focus more on the “secondary” type of analysis, where raw data had been transform into some sort of tabular format that fits in R so that I can start answering biological questions. > > p.s I can be a little slow to respond (work load etc) please don’t hold against me, I’ll always answer given a little time. cheers !

David Dilworth (20:44:49): > @David Dilworth has joined the channel

Kurt Showmaker (20:45:26): > @Kurt Showmaker has joined the channel

2020-07-24

Isha Goel (07:43:47): > @Isha Goel has joined the channel

Ruth Schmidt (10:45:03): > Hello everyone:wave:excited to meet you all virtually! My name isRuthand I’m a microbial ecologist and data scientist in the making. Currently, I am a postdoc between INRS (National Institute of Scientific Research) and the data analytic and dataviz company Plotly in Montreal, Canada. I’m developing Dash apps in R for omics data in microbiome research and this is my first time presenting at BioC. Looking forward to sharing how to build dash apps and showing some examples:hugging_face:I’m also looking for opportunities after my postdoc. Happy to connect and chat about projects & opportunities! - File (GIF): hello_bioc.gif

mirna (14:34:32) (in thread): > Hello Ruth, I was wondering if you know any metagenome binning package in R?

Rajiv Kumar Tripathi (14:52:04): > @Rajiv Kumar Tripathi has joined the channel

dnusskern (14:59:20): > @dnusskern has joined the channel

Krutika (16:03:20) (in thread): > Hi Nur-Taz! We developed annoFuse because we saw a need for a fusion filtering and annotation framework that is not currently available as a tool/package. We provide users the ability to filter fusion by read support/read-throughs or if they are present in normal databases as well as annotate fusion calls as found in TCGA and gene partners as Oncogene/TSG/Kinase etc . In addition we provide summary plots and domain level plots for visualization and review.

Ruth Schmidt (19:18:06) (in thread): > hey mirna! haven’t used it myself but this one seems like something you might wanna check out:gbtools

2020-07-26

Mirko Signorelli (11:35:42): > Hi everyone! My name isMirkoand I am a biostatistician who has been programming in R for a while now. I work as postdoc at the Leiden University Medical Center (in:flag-nl:) and my research deals with methods for the analysis of biomedical and omic data. This is my first Bioc conference and I am really enjoying it! I’m glad to have finally found out that there is such an active community behind Bioconductor:slightly_smiling_face:For full disclosure: so far I have uploadedmytwo R packagesto CRAN, but I am seriously considering uploading the next ones to Bioconductor! - Attachment (home): Mirko Signorelli, statistician and R programmer > Personal website of Mirko Signorelli, Postdoctoral researcher in Biostatistics at the Leiden University Medical Centre - Attachment (home): R packages - Mirko Signorelli > R packages and R code and data related to publications from Mirko Signorelli

Simina Boca (12:20:52): > Hi@Mirko Signorelli! I also do some work in DMD. Let me know if you want to chat about it!

Nur M Shahir (13:06:53): > Hello! My name is Nur, I am a postdoctoral scholar at PSU! My research involves the microbiome and scRNA in lung disease

Matt Brauer (14:02:15): > Hello all. I’m Matt Brauer, head of Data Science at Maze Therapeutics. Maze is a startup biotech founded by such luminaries in the fields of functional genomics and genetics as Steve Elledge, Jonathan Weissman, Mark Daly, Sek Kathiresan and Aaron Gitler. We’re reducing a lot of their labs’ advances to practice, in the service of discovering meaningful medical advancements in difficult diseases. My group is looking for an accomplished computational biologist/bioinformatics scientist to join our team, and have a job posted on the job board. Please check it out!

Ayush Aggarwal (09:09:40): > Hi everyone! I’m Ayush Aggarwal, a 4th year PhD student at CSIR-Institute of Genomics and Integrative Biology, Delhi, India. I am an experimental biologist with a huge interest in bioinformatics. I work in the field of skin pigmentation, currently interested in understanding the mechanism and the relevance behind melanocyte cell state transitions in normal and pathological conditions.

koki (10:21:55): > Hello, everyone. > I’m Koki Tsuyuzaki, the developer of Bioconductor packages metaSeq, MeSH.db, MeSH.AOR.db, MeSH.PCR.db,MeSH.XXX.eg.db, MeSHDbi,LRBase.XXX.eg.db, LRBaseDbi, scTensor, and scTGIF. > I’m going to talk about my packages at BioC Asia 2020, so please check it out.https://biocasia2020.bioconductor.org - Attachment (biocasia2020.bioconductor.org): BioC Asia 2020 > Oct 17 - 18

CristinaChe (07:31:18): > Hello everybody! I am Cristina and I work as computational biologist in Milano, Italy, analyzing omics data from brain organoids as a model to study alterations of neurodevelopmental trajectories. I am also passionate about exploring datasets related to social and health issues. Happy to be following Bioc2020 these days!

sani (09:41:00): > @sani has joined the channel

Adrija Kalvisa (09:46:08): > @Adrija Kalvisa has joined the channel

sani (09:47:36): > Hi everyone! nice to e-meet you.

Riyue Sunny Bao (09:52:25): > Hello everyone!! I am Riyue Bao (Sunny) from Pittsburgh, I am co-directing a team of bioinformatics scientists in the UPMC Hillman Cancer Center. My group supports the translational research at the Cancer Center and we also collaborate with UPMC Children’s Hospital and U Pitt. I also have my own lab focusing on tumor resistance mechanisms in immunotherapy in Dept of Medicine. We are interested in developing new methods to integrate multi-omics data, imaging data, and clinical data to find new targets that can be used to guide clinical trial design and development. - also we are actively recruiting talents to join us!! DM me, find me on twitter @RiyueSunnyBao, or shoot me an emailbaor@upmc.edu. Open for coffee chat!!!!:slightly_smiling_face:

Riyue Sunny Bao (09:55:53): > This is my first #BioC conference and it is my favorite conference this year!! I was not able to join previous conferences but happy this year the BioC organizers moved it to the virtual platform so I can finally join:hugging_face:kudo to all the organizers, committees, and sponsors to make this happen!!!!

Jonelle Villar (11:15:48): > @Jonelle Villar has joined the channel

Bharati Mehani (11:33:33): > @Bharati Mehani has joined the channel

Riyue Sunny Bao (12:34:41): > hiiii folks I was inspired by the discussion from #BoF regarding the recog of bioinformatician career in academia vs industry. If you have time to check out the pollhttps://twitter.com/RiyueSunnyBao/status/1288875156849528834 - Attachment (twitter): Attachment > As #bioinformatician, do you feel your #contribution is #recognized in your current work environment? > > Inspired by comments from #BioC2020 #BoF session and very curious on how the poll looks like! > > @AcademicChatter #AcademicChatter #AcademicTweeter #research #careeradvice

Andreas Gschwind (12:53:36): > @Andreas Gschwind has joined the channel

Brice Sarver (12:57:43): > @Brice Sarver has joined the channel

Saulius Lukauskas (12:59:30): > @Saulius Lukauskas has joined the channel

Andreas Gschwind (13:05:12): > Hi, I’m a computational biology postdoc at Stanford University. I mostly work on single-cell sequencing data, in particular Perturb-seq type screens for functional genomics elements. Previously I worked in human and population genetics, where I investigated expression and chromatin quantitative trait loci. Currently I’m interested in developing computational methods for novel experimental approaches and making them available to the community. My first R-package TAPseq (shameless plug, sorry ;)) was accepted to Bioconductor this cycle, so I would still consider myself a Bioconductor novice. I’m really enjoying the BioC2020 conference, thanks for organizing such a great meeting!

Matt McElheron (08:35:19): > Hey all, I am Matt McElheron, a young genetics and computational immunology PhD student from Ireland:face_with_cowboy_hat:I am interested in using machine learning and genetic/epigenetic data to identify patterns of biological ageing:dna::mag:I am excited to learn from everyone else and to connect with like-minded researchers on socials

Adit Wiryadana (08:36:55): > @Adit Wiryadana has joined the channel

Liisa Veerus (she/her) (08:38:32): > Hi all, I am Liisa, a postdoctoral fellow in Blaser Lab, working on the associations between microbiome, hormones, and health. Nice to meet you! Let’s connect on Twitterhttps://twitter.com/LiisaVeerus - Attachment (twitter.com): Dr Liisa Veerus (she/her) (@LiisaVeerus) | Twitter > The latest Tweets from Dr Liisa Veerus (she/her) (@LiisaVeerus). Postdoctoral fellow in @BlaserLab @RutgersCABM, working on microbiome • #scicomm & #sciart enthusiast :microbe::art: • All doodles are my own, please tag :female-scientist::skin-tone-3::flag-ee:. Oxford

Zachary DeBruine (16:19:14): > @Zachary DeBruine has joined the channel

Zachary DeBruine (16:38:31): > Wow Bioconductor has a slack channel with awesome community discussion?! Way to go. Zach DeBruine here from the Van Andel Institute, Michigan. I work on high-performance Non-negative Matrix Factorization (RcppML R package) and atlas-scale single-cell experiment data wrangling (ah, shall we perhaps make this a Bioconductor package?).https://www.zachdebruine.com/ - Attachment (zachdebruine.com): Zach DeBruine > Postdoctoral Fellow in Bioinformatics

Microglia1983 (19:58:19): > @Microglia1983 has joined the channel

2021-11-21

Vince Carey (15:55:21) (in thread): > Hi Zach – very nice post on NMF. What is the source of the aml data? Has it been published?

2021-11-23

Tyler Gorrie-Stone (09:30:38): > @Tyler Gorrie-Stone has joined the channel

Heather Bouzek (17:15:25): > @Heather Bouzek has joined the channel

Zachary DeBruine (19:25:21) (in thread): > Hi Vince, the AML data is yet unpublished and part of a larger work in progress, along with new methods for data integration with NMF.

2021-11-24

Helge Hecht (13:08:38): > @Helge Hecht has joined the channel

Helge Hecht (13:14:48): > Hi all! I’m developing mass spectrometry pipelines in R and python and would like to learn more about Bioconductor and eventually move our packages there or make them compliant. We currently publish things in bioconda, but interfacing with the Bioconductor ecosystem would be nice! > > I’d also be interested in getting involved in the review processes after publishing the first packages.

2021-11-29

2021-12-07

Castro Mbithi (02:28:51): > @Castro Mbithi has joined the channel

Castro Mbithi (02:40:08): > Hi, I am Castro, and I look forward to learning a lot from this group. I’m a developer atLD Talent, a project born out of Stanford University. It’s a tool that helps you find diverse vetted developers and helps you manage them in an efficient and transparent way.Free Trial Details: The platform gives you~5 free hoursto try them before hiring them forcontract or full-timeroles. To see how incredibly unique LD is, check out these video clips: > * [~1m] anoverviewof the platform and vetting > * [~30s][transparent work sessions](https://www.youtube.com/embed/iuEKqJbm9O8?start=832&end=865) > * [~1m][data on developers](https://www.youtube.com/embed/iuEKqJbm9O8?start=185&end=285), i.e. productivity and responsiveness > * [~3m][interviewing tips and time estimation](https://youtu.be/Ize-Gxaalgw?t=113) > * [~1m][budget control](https://www.youtube.com/embed/iuEKqJbm9O8?start=924&end=1007) > Diversity meets Capitalism: Our mission is to help teams become stronger by encouraging them to droplocation biases and constraintsthat unknowingly and implicitly exclude qualified, motivated, andefficientpeople of color from impactful roles they are perfect for. These knowledge workers are undervalued and overlooked by industry, creating an opportunity for a new type of global collaboration, given the rise ofremote distributed teamsin the new normal. > > We are always looking for BRUTALLY HONEST feedback :) - File (GIF): aldgif-2FPS.gif

2021-12-09

Garth Kong (20:17:13): > @Garth Kong has joined the channel

Garth Kong (20:27:52): > Hi all, my name is Garth and I am a computational biologist at Oregon Health and Science University! I use bioinformatics to study epigenetic changes in blood cancer. I’m looking to submit my first R package through Bioconductor in the second cycle (Oct 2022), and I look forward to signing up for the New Developer Program! > > Here is a link to my personal website and my lab:slightly_smiling_face:https://gartician.github.io/https://www.maxsonlab.org/ - Attachment (gartician.github.io): Garth Kong Personal Website > Your friendly research institute bioinformatic analyst - Attachment (maxsonlab.org): The Maxson Laboratory > The Maxson Lab. Studying the genomics of leukemia at the OHSU Knight Cancer Institute.

Thai Nguyen (20:45:06): > @Thai Nguyen has joined the channel

John Cheney (20:46:52): > @John Cheney has joined the channel

John Cheney (21:04:33): > Hi all! I work with@Garth Kongand our team (@Thai Nguyen,@Wesley Rosales,@Anjali Panikar) visualizing CITE-seq data in collaboration with the Maxson Lab @ OHSU. Pleased to be here with you all!

Wesley Rosales (21:09:18): > @Wesley Rosales has joined the channel

Anjali Panikar (22:49:27): > @Anjali Panikar has joined the channel

Jeanette Johnson (20:10:11): > @Jeanette Johnson has joined the channel

Jeanette Johnson (20:15:05): > Hi everybody, I’m a grad student in Elana Fertig’s lab at Johns Hopkins, right now I’m working on our NMF package, and my thesis is on TCR sequence analysis! I’m happy to be here and excited to learn from u all:relaxed:also happy to see some people from the PNW! I’m PNW born and bred, the east coast is cool but I miss the rain and the evergreens:evergreen_tree:

Amos Schorr (14:44:54): > @Amos Schorr has joined the channel

Amos Schorr (17:25:13): > Hey everyone! I’m launching a program with a nonprofit,Norn Group, to provide mentorship, bioinformatics training, and jobs to researchers around the world who are interested in the biology of aging and don’t have access to enough resources and opportunities. Excited to get to know some of y’all.

Alexandre Gouy (15:04:31): > Hi everyone! I’m Alex, I’m a bioinformatician by training and I’ve been using and developing Bioconductor resources for several years now. I’m now working for Tercen, bringing some BioC single cell pipelines into a user friendly data analysis platform. Feel free to ping me for a coffee IRL at BioC2022 or for a virtual chat!

Jennifer Holmes (16:08:32): > @Jennifer Holmes has joined the channel

Jennifer Holmes (16:13:47): > Hi everyone! I’m Jennifer - You can call me Jenn. I work for the CDC Foundation and I’m stationed at the Ohio Department of Health as a Bioinformatician. I’ve been using Bioconductor since I started my Masters back in 2015 and I ended up falling in love with R as I finished my program and started working in public health. I’m excited to be in the community and continue to improve my skills!:slightly_smiling_face:

Camille Santistevan (09:36:24): > @Camille Santistevan has joined the channel

Jennifer Foltz (10:50:15): > Hello all! I am an Instructor at Washington University in St. Louis! Background as a wet-lab immunologist, and for the last 3+ years doing single-cell analysis:computer:on samples from immunotherapy clinical trials! Happy to be here:nerd_face:

Matti Ruuskanen (11:20:48): > Hello! I’m a postdoctoral researcher in@Leo Lahti’s group at University of Turku. Mainly working on gut and skin microbiomes, but I’m also interested in environmental microbiology. Method-wise, I’m dealing with multiomic data integration, machine learning, and spatial modeling. I’ve worked mostly with R since 2013, dabbled in python and now getting started with Julia. In my spare time I play music, sing in a choir and do various sports. Send me a message if you want to connect!

Madelyn Carlson (16:17:00) (in thread): > Welcome, Emmanuel! Great to meet you. Quick plug to join the Slack#outreachychannel if you haven’t done so already:tada:

Madelyn Carlson (16:18:14) (in thread): > Welcome, Khadijah! My name is Madelyn, great to meet you. Quick plug to join the Slack#outreachychannel if you haven’t done so already:tada:

Madelyn Carlson (16:18:50) (in thread): > Amazing ~ welcome, Reece! My name is Madelyn, great to meet you. Quick plug to join the Slack#outreachychannel if you haven’t done so already:tada:

Madelyn Carlson (16:22:10): > Welcome, Outreachy applicants!:tada:My name is Madelyn, pronouns she/her. We’re excited for this contribution period and to meet y’all. I’ve been working with Jen and Paul on the Sweave2Rmd project. If you have any questions about our contribution period, come take a look at the information pinned our#outreachychannel, which includes guidance on contribution tasks and information on upcoming office hours with members of our team. Talk more soon!

Madelyn Carlson (16:43:25) (in thread): > :tada:

Madelyn Carlson (17:08:25) (in thread): > absolutely!

Sweta Kumari (09:53:41): > Hello everyone this is Sweta Kumari, after going through Bioconductor project description i really find it very interesting particularly the Microbiome Study Curation, it matches with my interest. I really want to know about human microbiome so that we can protect ourself from future pandemic or health related disease. I am hoping to learn lots of things while doing this internship project.

Maryam Gbemisola (10:35:39) (in thread): > Thank you

Madelyn Carlson (10:39:31) (in thread): > Welcome Maryam, great to meet you! If you haven’t done so already, feel free to join#outreachySlack channel to learn more about the Sweave2Rmd project or the#bugsigdbto learn more about the Microbiome Study Curation project:tada:

Madelyn Carlson (10:40:00) (in thread): > Welcome Vivian, great to meet you! If you haven’t done so already, feel free to join#outreachySlack channel to learn more about the Sweave2Rmd project or the#bugsigdbto learn more about the Microbiome Study Curation project:tada:

Madelyn Carlson (10:40:38) (in thread): > Welcome Kritika, great to e-meet you! If you haven’t done so already, feel free to join#outreachySlack channel to learn more about the Sweave2Rmd project or the#bugsigdbto learn more about the Microbiome Study Curation project:tada:

Madelyn Carlson (10:41:21) (in thread): > Welcome Violet, great to e-meet you! If you haven’t done so already, feel free to join#outreachySlack channel to learn more about the Sweave2Rmd project or the#bugsigdbto learn more about the Microbiome Study Curation project:tada:

Ufuoma Ejite (10:41:32): > @Ufuoma Ejite has joined the channel

Madelyn Carlson (10:41:42) (in thread): > Welcome Esther, great to e-meet you! If you haven’t done so already, feel free to join#outreachySlack channel to learn more about the Sweave2Rmd project or the#bugsigdbto learn more about the Microbiome Study Curation project:tada:

Madelyn Carlson (10:42:18) (in thread): > Welcome Brian, great to e-meet you! If you haven’t done so already, feel free to join#outreachySlack channel to learn more about the Sweave2Rmd project or the#bugsigdbto learn more about the Microbiome Study Curation project:tada:

Madelyn Carlson (10:43:10) (in thread): > Welcome Ameerah! Give me a shout if you need any help finding the#bugsigdbchannel (re: our other thread)

Madelyn Carlson (10:43:39) (in thread): > Welcome Aayushi, great to e-meet you! If you haven’t done so already, feel free to join#outreachySlack channel to learn more about the Sweave2Rmd project or the#bugsigdbto learn more about the Microbiome Study Curation project:tada:

Madelyn Carlson (10:46:21) (in thread): > Welcome Maryam, great to e-meet you! Wonderful questions - there are 2 projects that you can learn more about: the Sweave2Rmd project (search the#outreachySlack channel for more) and the Microbiome Study Curation project (join the#bugsigdbfor more information/space to ask questions). On both channels, you can find links to our issues containing information and instructions on the project and contribution tasks. Reach out and tag me if you have any questions!

Esther (10:49:08) (in thread): > Thank you Madelyn. > > I already joined the#bugsigdbchannel. > > Please whats the next step after joining the project channel?

Maryam Gbemisola (10:49:43) (in thread): > Thank you@Madelyn Carlson

Madelyn Carlson (10:50:35) (in thread): > Amazing and welcome Sweta! Great to e-meet you. If you haven’t done so already, you can join the#bugsigdbSlack channel to learn more about the Microbiome Study Curation project. You can also use that channel to ask questions about the project and its contribution tasks. In case it’s useful, here is the link to theBugSigDB-Outreachy contribution guidanceissues pg (this can be found pinned in the#bugsigdbchannel as well) - Attachment: #1 Microbiome Study Curation - Outreachy Applicant Instructions > Welcome Outreachy Applicants! > > Note: This task can be completed by multiple Outreachy applicants. > > BugSigDB Project > > BugSigDB is a wiki built to capture standardized microbial signatures of health outcomes and enables the systematic comparison and synthesis of published human microbiome studies, https://bugsigdb.org/Main_Page. As context, the human microbiome is the collection of microorganisms colonizing the human body. It performs essential functions such as producing vitamins and important nutrients, metabolizing food and drugs and toxins, regulating epithelial development, and influencing innate immunity. It is modified by diet, medical treatment, and host-to-host transmission, and can be a mediator between environment and health. The main result of most published human microbiome studies are lists or “signatures” of differentially abundant microbial taxa in different groups, which are often classified by health condition, behavior or risk factor. A microbial signature is a list of bacteria, viruses, and/or archaea reported to be differentially abundant with respect to health outcomes (e.g., cancer, diabetes, obesity) or with respect to sociodemographic characteristics (e.g., age, smoking, income, or place of residence). These signatures are often reported in heterogeneous ways with different taxonomic nomenclature databases and do not allow direct comparison of methods or results in different studies. BugSigDB contributors aim to create a comprehensive coverage of the human microbiome literature that allows for (1) comparisons between new research findings and previously published results in any area of the health literature and (2) identify common patterns of differentially abundant microbes across otherwise disparate health outcomes. > > Prep Work > > To make a contribution to the BugSigDB project, applicants should first review the onboarding materials:
> BugSigDB project information, estimated time: 15 minutes (link)
> Reading materials on the human microbiome, estimated time: 2 hours (link)
> Step-by-step guidance on how to document a signature in BugSigDB (link)
> View examples of completed BugSigDB curations, estimated time: 15 minutes (link)
> Video trainings on BugSigDB curation (link) > > Contribution Task > > Review article on the human microbiome that is heavily annotated by BugSigDB project leaders (e.g., explaining the section(s) of the article that convey study design and the specific characteristics that determine study design) and complete a mini-curation, akin to BugSigDB curations, on Qualtrics XM. Link to curation form: https://cunysph.az1.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_0cYerh299Ftl49M. > > (Optional) Stretch Task: As an optional, higher-difficulty-level task, you may claim an article on the human microbiome from the BugSigDBcuration Github issues tagged “paper to curate” and work on a full curation. > > To claim an article for curation, post a response to the issue with your name and we will mark it as claimed. > > To curate the article you select, first create a BugSigDB account (https://bugsigdb.org/Special:RequestAccount) and follow the website prompts to complete the curation. > > Questions > > Communications regarding specific tasks should be done on the Slack BioC channel, to join, view https://slack.bioconductor.org/ then search for the channel #bugsigdb > > Contributions > > Remember to record your contributions in Outreachy at https://www.outreachy.org/outreachy-may-2023-internship-cohort/communities/bioconductor/#microbiome-study-curation|https://www.outreachy.org/outreachy-may-2023-internship-cohort/communities/bioconductor/#microbiome-study-curation.

Atrayee Samanta (02:10:53): > @Atrayee Samanta has joined the channel

Monalika Patnaik (02:32:18) (in thread): > Hey@MaryJones:wave:For first contribution we need to understand the basics of R and R Markdown..you can work on this issue as your first contribution task:blush:https://github.com/Bioconductor/sweave2rmd/issues/62 - Attachment: #62 Sweave2Rmd Knowledge Check > Welcome Outreachy applicants! > > Note: Multiple Outreachy applicants can work on this issue. > > Please choose this as your first contribution task to the Bioconductor Sweave2Rmd project as it will familiarize you with the R, R Markdown, GitHub processes, documentation, and the Sweave2Rmd conversion process (learn more about Bioconductor here). > > Sweave2Rmd Project > > Sweave2Rmd is a community project to convert Sweave vignettes to Rmd. Currently, there are approximately 900 vignettes .Rnw in software packages. These vignettes do not operate as effectively as .Rmd as they are older, combine latex with R, and compile to PDF whereas .Rmd vignettes compile to HTML, are BiocStyle compatible, and easier to read and maintain. Therefore, the aim of the Sweave2Rmd project is to convert these Sweave vignettes to .Rmd. > > Prep Work > > If you’re not familiar with R and R Markdown, please learn a little about them. The following links should be sufficient to get you started: R Primer - Programming basics or R Markdown. Then you may complete the following steps: > > • Install R > • Install RStudio > • In RStudio or through the terminal, install devtools and BiocManager:
> install.packages(c("devtools", "BiocManager")) > • Read through the Sweave2Rmd Contribute article, which explains step-by-step the process of converting a Sweave document to Rmd. The article will be important for completing this contribution task. > > Contribution Task > > After reading the Sweave2Rmd Contribute article, you may complete the 2023 Outreachy Sweave2Rmd Contribution Familiarity Quiz. The quiz contains questions about the process of converting a Sweave vignette to Rmd. It is an opportunity to both test your familiarity with the Contribute article and learn how to convert a vignette if you choose to complete the second (optional) contribution task. We expect the quiz to take up to 3 hours to complete. If you have any questions about the quiz, feel free to post on the Sweave2Rmd Outreachy Slack channel. To join the Slack channel, view https://slack.bioconductor.org/ then search for the channel #outreachy. > > Remember to record your contributions in Outreachy at https://www.outreachy.org/outreachy-may-2023-internship-cohort/communities/bioconductor/convert-sweave-vignettes-to-r-markdown/contributions/

Talk soon!

2023-03-29

Matteo Tiberti (10:05:17): > hi, I’m Matteo, staff scientist at the Danish Cancer Society. We are about to submit one (or two, if we make it!) package for evaluation for the inclusion in BioC, so I thought I’d say hello. From the BioC GitHub I understand there might be a specific channel for that? Thank you and happy computing everyone!:computer:

Lori Shepherd (10:08:17) (in thread): > BioC GitHub? If you mean package development and potential access I would recommend the#developers-forum,#bioc_git,#bioc-buildsand potentially#github-actionscheers

Matteo Tiberti (10:11:09) (in thread): > sorry - I mean, from the template text for the submission in the Contributions GitHub repohttps://github.com/Bioconductor/Contributions/issues/new- “please use the#package-submissionchannel of our Community Slack”. Thank you, I’ll try join those as well!

Lori Shepherd (10:11:55) (in thread): > ah yes. well glad to have you apart of the community:slightly_smiling_face:

Matteo Tiberti (10:20:53) (in thread): > thank you! Happy to be here:smile:

Louis Le Nézet (03:17:34): > @Louis Le Nézet has joined the channel

Louis Le Nézet (03:28:09): > Hi everyone ! > My name is Louis Le Nézet and I’m working in genetic. I’ve never contribute before to Bioconductor but I motivated to do so. > I have one package that was on Cran, that could be of interest on Bioconductor (kinship2: pedigree functions). I’m also part of a group where we develop shiny modules that we would like to share as a bioconductor package. > What would be the best course of action ?

Lori Shepherd (07:30:02) (in thread): > There are a few difference from CRAN to Bioconductor –http://contributions.bioconductor.org/has a full run down but careful attention to the first few sections on “General BIoconductor Package Development” and “Important Bioconductor Package Development Features” –@Aedin Culhaneis there going to be another round of the mentorship program as that might also be a good experience if you already have the basics of packages set to go

Louis Le Nézet (08:29:58) (in thread): > I would be really interested in a mentorship program. > I have the some basics knowledge on packages but I would really appreciate some guidance.

Physalia-courses (06:13:21): > @Physalia-courses has joined the channel

Physalia-courses (06:27:08): > Hi all, we arePhysalia, a platform that specializes in providing high-quality training in Genomics, Bioinformatics, statistics and related disciplines. These courses are conducted by renowned experts and cover a wide range of topics, and our aim is to empower researchers with the tools and techniques necessary to excel in their respective fields. Nice to meet you all:wave:

Izabela Mamede (16:15:23): > Hello everyone! > My name is Izabela, I am a PhD Student at the Federal University of Minas Gerais (UFMG) in Brazil. I will be in person at Boston presenting a package demo on the end of the last day of BioC2023. Happy to connect and come to talk with me in person!

Esther Camacho (01:53:32): > Hi everyone! My name is Esther and I work as a bioinformatics technician at VHIR in Barcelona. I believe this conference will be a great opportunity to broaden my knowledge and to do net-working. Nice to meet you!

Madan Mohan (02:29:37): > @Madan Mohan has joined the channel

Kacper Żukowski (08:04:50): > @Kacper Żukowski has joined the channel

Mohamed S.AboHoussien (11:02:49): > @Mohamed S.AboHoussien has joined the channel

Mohamed S.AboHoussien (11:35:35): > Hello everyone nice to meet you all > My name is Mohamed Said Ahmed AboHussien, and I am very interested in becoming involved in research in the Metagenomics field. I have aBSc.inGenetics Agriculture College Al-Azhar University > I have taken the following courses related to Metagenomics : > I studied > 1. QIIME 2 Cancer Microbiome Intervention Tutorial > 2. Fecal microbiota transplant (FMT) study > 3.“Moving Pictures” tutorial - Multiple Interface Edition > 4.“Atacama soil microbiome” tutorial > 5. Parkinson’s Mouse Tutorial > 6. Differential abundance analysis with Gneiss > For all of the above, I used the Qiime2 command line interface (Bash) > Galaxy pipelines and workflows like > 1. Metagenomics data analyses > 2. Metatranscriptomics analysis > 3. Amplicon 16srRNA data analysis > 4. Shotgun metagenomics > 5. Taxonomic profiling and Visualization for Meta-genomics data. > 6. Assembly of Metagenomics sequence data > > I participated in three internships related to the Bioinformatics > 1.Galaxy Training Network GTN Smörgåsbord 3 > 2. Hack Bio Internship > 3.H3ABioNet Internship > > I have the following related experience > • Metatranscriptomics analysis using microbiome RNA-seq data > • Amplicon 16srRNA data analysis and visualization > • 16S Microbial Analysis with Mothur (short) > • AMR Antibiotic resistance detection > • Pathogen detection from (direct Nanopore) sequencing data using Galaxy –Foodborne Edition > • Taxonomic profiling and Visualization for Meta-genomics data > • Assembly of Metagenomics sequence data > • SARS_cov2 variant analysis > • Shotgun metagenomics reveals a wide array of antibiotic resistance genes and mobile elements in a polluted lake in India > • Efficient generation of complete sequences of MDR-encoding plasmids by rapid assembly of MinION barcoding sequencing data > • imported: Processed Indian, Sweden Datasets: Taxonomic classification and extraction of functional information

Mikaila Chetty (07:42:43): > Hi all! I’m Mikaila and work as a researcher/PhD candidate. I work on C. elegan model organism (everything from locomotion, behavior, oxidative stress, mitochondria assays, protein assays, transriptomics and molecular docking are all my kind of thing). My long term goals are to focus on Aging & Alzheimer’s in the biotech/pharma industry. I am looking forward to connecting with everyone. Also - all guidance and tips on the industry & career development are welcome :) > > Feel free to connect with me here: > • Twitter (https://twitter.com/mikaila_chetty_) > • LinkedIn (https://www.linkedin.com/in/mikailachetty/)

This is an exciting opportunity for me, and I look forward to making significant contributions. > > Here are my socials. I’d love to connect with youhttps://www.linkedin.com/in/enyinna-chinwendu-promise/

Nwajei Edgar (10:27:48) (in thread): > Welcome Greatman, nice to have you here… > > You can start here:https://community-bioc.slack.com/archives/C04RATV9VCY/p1696254316863939 - Attachment: Attachment > Welcome Outreachy applicants! We’re so excited to have you here for the contribution period. Please see this issue on Github for instructions on getting started: https://github.com/waldronlab/BugSigDBcuration/issues/94. If you have any questions, please post them in this channel so that all applicants can see the answers. Thank you so much for your interest and we look forward to working with you.

Odigiri Great Alume (14:37:18): > @Iram JamshedOnce you have identified the condition of study ( You can do that by reading the title of the annotated pdf) and going through the PDF to get context), for instance, if Breast Cancer is your disease of interest, the next thing is to search for it using the link provided on the survey. A list of other articles will pop. Merely looking at the titles of the articles, you can tell which one is closest to the article in the annotated pdf. That’s the one you choose. Then Fill in the information required of you. > > I hope this helps.@Iram Jamshed

Dimitri Meistermann (01:33:10) (in thread): > My first submission, a package named “markergrid”, is close to be ready. I just have a slight conflict between biocheck and roxygen that creates a file named R/markergrid-package.R and man/markergrid-package.Rd. As I understand those are created to manage namespace and create a global manpage to the package. My problem is that biocheck require that 80% of manpages should have a runnable example, and as the package is very small I have only one manpage for my function, so only 50% of .Rd files have examples. But man/markergrid-package.Rd doesn’t contain any function so it doesn’t make sense to me to include runnable examples. > > I’m not sure that introductions thread is the right place to ask, so I can move my question elsewhere.

Dimitri Meistermann (01:33:30) (in thread): > And thank you for proposing your help:slightly_smiling_face:

Lluís Revilla (03:46:37) (in thread): > Probably the best way is in slack are the channels#developers-forumor#developers-mentorship. But you could also use the bioc-devel mailing list, but for this short question I can answer here, and if you have more doubts you can ask somewhere else

Lluís Revilla (03:47:17) (in thread): > The namespace of the package is controlled by the file NAMESPACE. It is based on the#' @exporttags you use. > Yes, usually help pages for the package do not include examples. This can be explained to the reviewer and I’m sure they will understand. However, it is not normal for a package to have just a single function.

2023-10-07

Hodan Abdirisak (00:30:14) (in thread): > Warm welcoming,@Sophie Sorensonyour contribution will be easy and smoothly follow this where you get instructions about how to make first contributionhttps://github.com/waldronlab/BugSigDBcuration/issues/94and you can ask help to us every time you need - Attachment: #94 Welcome Outreachy Applicants! > Welcome Outreachy Applicants! > > Note: This task can be completed by multiple Outreachy applicants. > > BugSigDB Project > > BugSigDB is a wiki built to capture standardized microbial signatures of health outcomes and enables the systematic comparison and synthesis of published human microbiome studies, https://bugsigdb.org/Main_Page. As context, the human microbiome is the collection of microorganisms colonizing the human body. It performs essential functions such as producing vitamins and important nutrients, metabolizing food and drugs and toxins, regulating epithelial development, and influencing innate immunity. It is modified by diet, medical treatment, and host-to-host transmission, and can be a mediator between environment and health. The main result of most published human microbiome studies are lists or “signatures” of differentially abundant microbial taxa in different groups, which are often classified by health condition, behavior or risk factor. A microbial signature is a list of bacteria, viruses, and/or archaea reported to be differentially abundant with respect to health outcomes (e.g., cancer, diabetes, obesity) or with respect to sociodemographic characteristics (e.g., age, smoking, income, or place of residence). These signatures are often reported in heterogeneous ways with different taxonomic nomenclature databases and do not allow direct comparison of methods or results in different studies. BugSigDB contributors aim to create a comprehensive coverage of the human microbiome literature that allows for (1) comparisons between new research findings and previously published results in any area of the health literature and (2) identify common patterns of differentially abundant microbes across otherwise disparate health outcomes. > > Prep Work > > To make a contribution to the BugSigDB project, applicants should first review the onboarding materials:
> BugSigDB project information, estimated time: 15 minutes (link)
> Reading materials on the human microbiome, estimated time: 2 hours (link)
> Step-by-step guidance on how to document a signature in BugSigDB (link)
> View examples of completed BugSigDB curations, estimated time: 15 minutes (link)
> Video trainings on BugSigDB curation (link) > > First Contribution Task > > 1. Go to https://cunysph.az1.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_7UTYhqDc3qYwiEu > 2. Review the article on the human microbiome that has been heavily annotated by BugSigDB project leaders (e.g., explaining the section(s) of the article that convey study design and the specific characteristics that determine study design) > 3. Complete a mini-curation, akin to BugSigDB curations, on Qualtrics XM. > > Link to curation form: https://cunysph.az1.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_7UTYhqDc3qYwiEu > > Please submit a fully completed mini-curation via the form above before continuing to the second contribution task. > > Second Contribution Task (Optional) > > As an optional, higher-difficulty-level task, you may claim an article on the human microbiome from the BugSigDBcuration Github issues tagged “paper to curate” and work on a full curation. > > To claim an article for curation, post a response to the issue with your name and we will mark it as claimed. Please only claim one article for curation until you have completed this curation and it has been graded. > > To curate the article you select, first create a BugSigDB account (https://bugsigdb.org/Special:RequestAccount) and follow the website prompts to complete the curation. > > Assessment & Grading > > Your first contribution will be scored for accuracy. Your second contribution will be scored with the following rubric: > > 1. All elements marked “Needs review” (none “Incomplete”) (1 point): > 2. Correct study design (1 point): > 3. Entered all relevant experiments and no irrelevant experiments (1 point): > 4. Body site correctly identified (i.e. does not include multiple sites) (1 point): > 5. Condition entered according to contrast (correct EFO ontology) (1 point): > 6. Contrast groups correctly identified (1 point): > 7. Groups correctly labeled as 1 and 0 (1=cases, 0=controls) (1 point): > 8. Antibiotic exclusion correctly identified (1 point): > 9. Correctly identified sequencing details (1 point for sequencing type and variable region, 1 point for sequencing platform) (2 points): > 10. Identified correct statistical test (1 point): > 11. Identified MHT correction (1 point): > 12. Correctly recorded matched on variables (1 point): > 13. Entered correct number of statistical tests per experiment (1 point): > 14. All diversity measures identified (1 point): > 15. Diversity results correctly entered as increased/ decreased/ unchanged (1 point): > 16. All signature sources correctly identified (-1 for each error) (2 points): > 17. Abundance direction correctly selected (1 point): > 18. Members of signatures identified correctly (1 point for single small error, 0 points for anything more. Incorrect means missing or extraneous taxon) (2 points): > 19. Correct use of NCBI taxonomy (don’t deduct if can’t easily find correct taxon in NCBI taxonomy database. 1 for one error, 0 for multiple errors) (2 points): > > Total (maximum 24 points): > > Please note it may take us several days to score your second contribution, depending on how many contributions we receive. Once scored, you will have the opportunity to revise your curation to remedy any identified errors; however, we will record both your original and revised score when evaluating your second contribution. We strongly recommend you ask questions on our Slack channel, compare your curation to completed curations on BugSigDB, and attend office hours prior to submitting your second contribution for grading. > > Questions > > Communications regarding specific tasks should be done on the Slack BioC channel, to join, view https://slack.bioconductor.org/ then search for the channel #bugsigdb > > To reduce duplicate questions, please do not directly message or email the BugSigDB mentors. > > Office Hours > > Office hours will be held at 10 AM EDT (eastern daylight time) every Thursday at: https://us02web.zoom.us/j/2737200499. Attendance by all applicants is highly encouraged. Find out the time in your local time zone here: https://www.timeanddate.com/worldclock/converter.html?iso=20230929T140000&p1=179 > > Contributions > > Remember to record your contributions in Outreachy at https://www.outreachy.org/outreachy-december-2023-internship-round/communities/bioconductor/microbiome-study-curation.

2023-10-08

Odigiri Great Alume (02:17:09): > Good morning guys. > So I need clarification on something please:pleading_face:I’m working on “Lower Respiratory Tract microbiome composition and smokers behavior”
> > The study subjects were grouped into three: Never smokers (NS), Active Smokers (AS) and Former smokers (FS). > > The study employed the Prospective cohort study design. > > Please how do I place these subjects into GROUP 1 and GROUP 0 seeing that they are three groups (NF, AF, FS)?:thinking_face:

Odigiri Great Alume (02:19:08): - File (JPEG): Screenshot_20231007-005253.jpg

Chioma Onyido (02:37:41) (in thread): > Hi@Odigiri Great AlumeI think one thing is to check the groups that were contrasted during the differential abundance analysis of the microbial taxa.

Chioma Onyido (02:44:11) (in thread): > Group 0 is usually the control/ reference group, while Group 1 is the “case”, for example, if the study is about AD, Group 0 is the normal group, I.e those without AD, while Group 1 would be those with AD. > > But sometimes it could get tricky. > In your case, maybe differential abundance was reported for NS vs AS and FS vs AS. > In this case you might have to create separate experiments for both. A clue is to check the figures, focus more on the table/ figure where the differential abundant taxa was reported, that should give you an idea. > > I hope this helps.

Odigiri Great Alume (04:00:11) (in thread): > Thanks dear. I will look now

Tim Treis (10:48:09): > @Tim Treis has left the channel

Odigiri Great Alume (14:49:52) (in thread): > Alright@Andres WokatyI will
